heroOverview: Diphyllobothriasis is a foodborne cestode infection caused by Diphyllobothrium latum and related tapeworm species — the longest parasites capable of infecting humans, reaching 10-15 metres in length — acquired by eating raw or undercooked freshwater fish. An estimated 9-20 million people are infected worldwide, with the highest burdens in Scandinavia, northern Russia, eastern Europe, the Great Lakes region of North America, Chile, and parts of Japan and East Africa. Most infections are asymptomatic or produce only mild gastrointestinal complaints, but Diphyllobothrium latum is unique among intestinal tapeworms in its capacity to absorb vitamin B12 from the host's intestine, causing megaloblastic anaemia — the so-called 'fish tapeworm anaemia' — in a clinically significant minority of heavily infected patients. Praziquantel is highly effective in a single dose, and the prognosis is excellent with prompt treatment.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 9-20 million people infected worldwide (systematic review data)
keyStats.demographics: Historically concentrated in Scandinavia, Russia, and eastern Europe; increasing cases reported in North America, Chile, and East Asia linked to the sushi and sashimi food culture; all ages affected
keyStats.avgAge: No strict age peak; adults who regularly eat raw fish are at highest risk; cases arise across the lifespan
keyStats.globalCases: More than 14 Diphyllobothrium species can infect humans; D. latum is the most prevalent; D. nihonkaiense is the leading species in Japan and Korea

definition: Diphyllobothriasis (ICD-10: B70.0) is a human cestode infection caused by pseudophyllidean tapeworms of the genus Diphyllobothrium, most commonly Diphyllobothrium latum (the broad or fish tapeworm). The life cycle requires two intermediate hosts: copepod crustaceans (first intermediate host) and freshwater fish — pike, perch, salmon, trout, burbot — in which procercoid larvae develop into plerocercoid (sparganum) larvae in the muscle. Humans and fish-eating mammals (bears, seals, dogs) are definitive hosts who become infected by eating raw or poorly cooked infected fish. After ingestion, the plerocercoid develops in the small intestine into an adult tapeworm attached to the jejunal mucosa by its scolex. D. latum adults may survive for 20 years or more and shed millions of eggs daily in the faeces. The unique pathological feature of D. latum compared to Taenia tapeworms is its highly efficient absorption of cobalamin (vitamin B12) from the host's ileum, which in heavy infections creates a functional B12 deficiency and megaloblastic macrocytic anaemia indistinguishable from pernicious anaemia. The condition falls under infectious disease and gastroenterology. Saltwater fish species do not harbour Diphyllobothrium; marine fish like salmon can be infected if they return to freshwater spawning grounds. The global spread of raw fish cuisine (sushi, sashimi, gravlax, ceviche using freshwater species) has dispersed cases from their traditional boreal and subarctic endemic zones.

typesClassification.description: The most prevalent species worldwide, transmitted by pike, perch, and other northern freshwater fish; the species most strongly associated with vitamin B12 deficiency and megaloblastic anaemia.
The dominant species in Japan, Korea, and the North Pacific; transmitted by Pacific salmon and trout; rarely causes B12 deficiency.
A species associated with subarctic lakes and implicated in clusters of cases in Scandinavia and the Great Lakes region; typically lighter infection and fewer clinical manifestations.
Over 14 species can infect humans; most are clinically similar to each other and managed with the same praziquantel regimen.

primarySymptoms: Passage of proglottid segments in the stool — the most alarming and often the first symptom prompting a patient to seek care; segments are expelled singly or in chains and may visibly move.
Vague abdominal discomfort or cramping — a mild, poorly localised epigastric or periumbilical ache from tapeworm attachment and motility in the small intestine; usually low-grade.
Diarrhoea or alternating loose stools and constipation — disruption of normal intestinal peristalsis from the large worm mass in the jejunum.
Nausea and anorexia — reported in approximately 20-30% of symptomatic patients, especially early in infection.
Fatigue and weakness — in lighter infections, fatigue may be the only symptom; in heavier infections it reflects B12 deficiency and anaemia.
Megaloblastic (macrocytic) anaemia — pallor, breathlessness, and profound fatigue in the minority of patients with marked B12 depletion; characteristic of heavy D. latum infection.

Neurological symptoms of B12 deficiency (subacute combined degeneration) — tingling and numbness in the extremities, gait instability, and cognitive changes when B12 deficiency is severe and prolonged; this is a rare but serious complication.
Weight loss — in heavy long-standing infections, nutritional competition and malabsorption contribute to gradual weight loss.
Urticaria and pruritus — immune-mediated skin reactions during early infection.
Abdominal distension and bloating — from flatulence generated by worm metabolism and altered intestinal fermentation.
earlyWarningSigns: Noticing a pale, ribbon-like segment or chain of segments in the toilet after defecation.
Unexplained macrocytic anaemia (large red blood cells) on a routine blood count in someone who eats raw fish regularly.
Mild intermittent abdominal discomfort with no other cause identified in a person from a fish tapeworm endemic region.
An unexplained low serum vitamin B12 level in the absence of dietary B12 restriction or gastric disease.
emergencySigns: Subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord — progressive paraesthesia, limb weakness, and gait disturbance from prolonged untreated B12 deficiency, which requires urgent B12 replacement and tapeworm treatment.
Severe megaloblastic anaemia with haemoglobin below 7 g/dL causing cardiac decompensation, breathlessness at rest, or angina requiring transfusion.
Intestinal obstruction from a large worm mass: acute abdominal pain, distension, and cessation of stool passage.
Cholangitis or pancreatitis from proglottid migration into the bile or pancreatic duct — rare but documented.

causes.cause: Eating raw or undercooked freshwater fish
causes.description: The dominant route of infection. Freshwater fish — pike, perch, burbot, ruffe, trout, salmon passing through freshwater — harbour plerocercoid larvae in their muscle tissue. Traditional dishes including Scandinavian gravlax, Finnish mätijuusto, Eastern European carp dishes eaten raw or lightly cured, and Pacific salmon sashimi are well-documented sources.

causes.cause: Tasting raw fish preparations during cooking
causes.description: Cooks who taste raw fish or fish-containing mixtures (e.g., fish cakes, gefilte fish, raw salmon tartare) before cooking are exposed to viable plerocercoid larvae, accounting for a disproportionate share of cases among food preparers.

causes.cause: Inadequate freezing or curing of fish
causes.description: Salt curing, light smoking, and marination in acid (lime or vinegar) do not reliably kill Diphyllobothrium plerocercoids in fish muscle. Freezing at −20°C for 24-48 hours or cooking to internal temperature above 63°C is required for reliable inactivation.

causes.cause: Consumption of sushi and sashimi containing freshwater or migratory fish
causes.description: Global spread of raw fish cuisine has introduced diphyllobothriasis to countries far outside the traditional boreal endemic zone. Pacific salmon and related species used in sushi are increasingly implicated; D. nihonkaiense is associated with Pacific salmon sashimi in Japan, Korea, and North America.

causes.cause: Environmental persistence of eggs in freshwater
causes.description: Eggs shed into waterways from human and animal faeces (bears, seals, dogs) infect copepods, which are eaten by fish, sustaining the environmental cycle. In lakes with heavy fish-eating mammal populations, fish infection rates can be very high, especially in pike and perch.

riskFactors.factor: Eating raw or lightly cured freshwater fish
riskFactors.description: The single most important risk factor. Cooking fish to 63°C internal temperature or freezing at −20°C for 24-48 hours eliminates risk entirely. Gravlax, marinated carp, raw salmon, and freshwater fish tartares are the most commonly implicated dishes.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in or travel to endemic regions
riskFactors.description: Finland, Sweden, Russia, Baltic states, Poland, and Chile have historically high infection rates. North American Great Lakes communities and North Pacific salmon-consuming populations are at moderate risk.

riskFactors.factor: Home food preparation of raw fish
riskFactors.description: Tasting raw fish during preparation of traditional recipes is a documented independent risk factor, particularly among home cooks who may not appreciate the risk of undercooked fish.
Individuals who are vegetarian or vegan, or have low B12 intake for other reasons, have smaller B12 reserves and reach symptomatic deficiency more quickly with a heavy tapeworm burden than omnivores.
Children who participate in fish-handling or traditional food preparation may be exposed early; cerebral or spinal complications of B12 deficiency can be devastating at a young age.

riskFactors.factor: Consumption of commercially distributed raw salmon products
riskFactors.description: Commercial fish intended for raw consumption should be certified as freeze-treated at regulatory standards. Non-compliant products, particularly artisan or home-prepared cured salmon, remain a source of infection.

affectedDemographics: Adults in Scandinavia, Russia, Poland, and the Baltic states where raw and lightly cured fish is traditional
Jewish communities in North America and Israel who prepare gefilte fish using traditional raw carp tasting
Japanese, Korean, and North American Pacific Northwest populations who consume salmon sashimi frequently
Indigenous communities in subarctic Alaska, Canada, and Siberia who eat raw or fermented freshwater fish
Travellers who adopt local raw fish eating practices in endemic regions
Home cooks and professional chefs who routinely taste raw fish preparations
diagnosisOverview: Diphyllobothriasis is most often diagnosed when a patient reports passing pale, ribbon-like segments (proglottids) in the stool — a striking and alarming symptom that almost always prompts medical consultation. Confirmation rests on stool microscopy identifying the characteristic Diphyllobothrium eggs: ovoid, operculated, 55-75 µm × 40-50 µm, with a small abopercular knob; or on direct visualisation of proglottids, which are wider than they are long (distinguishing them from Taenia segments, which are longer than wide). Complete blood count may show macrocytic anaemia with hypersegmented neutrophils in patients with significant B12 depletion; serum vitamin B12 should be measured in all confirmed cases. Peripheral blood eosinophilia is typically absent or mild. Colonoscopy or upper endoscopy can occasionally identify the tapeworm scolex or segments in the bowel, but they are rarely required for diagnosis. The main diagnostic pitfall is confusing Diphyllobothrium eggs with those of other cestodes or operculated trematode eggs; laboratory expertise and clinical context are important. In countries where fish tapeworm infection is uncommon, requesting a specific stool ova and parasite examination with appropriate clinical context is essential, as routine stool cultures do not detect tapeworm eggs.

diagnosticTests.test: Stool microscopy for eggs and proglottids
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the characteristic ovoid operculated Diphyllobothrium eggs or expelled proglottid segments; the definitive diagnostic test.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A stool sample is collected at home and examined by wet-preparation or formalin-ether concentration microscopy. Multiple samples improve sensitivity. If proglottids are passed, bring a segment to the clinic in a container — this confirms the diagnosis at a glance.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects B12 depletion in patients with diphyllobothriasis, which occurs because D. latum competitively absorbs cobalamin from the ileum; essential in all confirmed cases to determine whether B12 replacement is needed.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw with results available within a few hours or overnight. Levels below 200 pg/mL confirm deficiency; values 200-400 pg/mL are borderline and should be followed with methylmalonic acid and homocysteine for functional assessment.

diagnosticTests.test: Complete blood count with peripheral smear
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects megaloblastic macrocytic anaemia (raised MCV, hypersegmented neutrophils) characteristic of significant B12 deficiency from heavy D. latum infection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; the peripheral blood smear is examined by a haematologist if macrocytosis or hypersegmented neutrophils are reported.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Functional markers of cellular B12 deficiency; both are elevated before haematological changes appear, allowing earlier identification of clinically significant B12 depletion in tapeworm-infected patients with borderline serum B12.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw; results take 24-48 hours depending on the laboratory.

diagnosticTests.test: Intrinsic factor antibodies and gastric parietal cell antibodies
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes pernicious anaemia, the most important differential diagnosis for macrocytic anaemia with low B12 in a patient not known to have fish tapeworm infection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw; part of a broader B12 deficiency workup.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Occasionally identifies the tapeworm scolex or worm body in the proximal small bowel; rarely required for diagnosis but can guide extraction if the worm is visible.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A sedated endoscopic procedure lasting 15-30 minutes; reserved for cases where stool examination is negative but clinical suspicion is high, or when endoscopic extraction is planned.

treatmentOverview: Praziquantel is the first-line treatment and is curative in a single oral dose of 10 mg/kg with a meal. The drug disrupts the tapeworm's calcium uptake, causing tegumental disintegration and worm expulsion within 24-48 hours; treatment success is defined by cessation of proglottid passage and negative stool examination at 4-6 weeks. An alternative is niclosamide 2 g as a single dose (chewed thoroughly), which was the historical standard before praziquantel became widely available and is still effective where praziquantel is not available; niclosamide is not widely marketed in all countries. Vitamin B12 supplementation is essential whenever serum B12 is low or megaloblastic anaemia is confirmed. In the absence of neurological symptoms, oral B12 (1,000-2,000 µg daily) is adequate after the tapeworm is eliminated because intrinsic factor secretion and gastric parietal cell function are normal — the deficiency resolves once B12 absorption is no longer being competed for by the worm. In patients with confirmed subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord or other neurological B12 sequelae, intramuscular hydroxocobalamin 1,000 µg daily for 7 days followed by weekly then monthly maintenance is required. Haemoglobin and B12 levels should be re-checked at 6-8 weeks to confirm haematological recovery. Intestinal obstruction from a large worm mass is rare and is managed conservatively with nasogastric suction and hydration in most cases; endoscopic or surgical intervention is reserved for failure of conservative management.

medicalTreatments.name: Praziquantel
medicalTreatments.description: First-line anthelmintic: a single oral dose of 10 mg/kg taken with a meal. Causes rapid calcium-dependent tegumental disintegration of the tapeworm scolex and segments, leading to worm expulsion within 24-48 hours. Well tolerated; mild dizziness and abdominal cramping may occur transiently.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates of approximately 90-95% after a single dose, confirmed by negative stool microscopy at 4-6 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Niclosamide
medicalTreatments.description: An older cestocidal drug given as a 2 g single dose, chewed and swallowed with water; inhibits oxidative phosphorylation in the tapeworm. Historical first-line before praziquantel; still used in some countries where praziquantel is unavailable.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates of approximately 85-90% after a single dose; somewhat less convenient than praziquantel because tablets must be chewed rather than swallowed whole.

medicalTreatments.name: Vitamin B12 supplementation (oral)
medicalTreatments.description: Oral cyanocobalamin or methylcobalamin 1,000-2,000 µg daily replenishes B12 stores after the tapeworm is eliminated. Appropriate when B12 is low but neurological complications are absent; the gut absorbs sufficient cobalamin via passive diffusion even without intrinsic factor at high doses.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Serum B12 normalises within 4-8 weeks of combined tapeworm treatment and oral replacement; haemoglobin recovers within 6-12 weeks in megaloblastic anaemia.

medicalTreatments.name: Intramuscular hydroxocobalamin
medicalTreatments.description: Given as daily injections of 1,000 µg for 7 days, then weekly, then monthly in patients with neurological complications of B12 deficiency (subacute combined degeneration). Bypasses any ongoing absorptive deficit and rapidly raises serum and tissue B12 levels.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Neurological improvement occurs in approximately 60-80% of patients with subacute combined degeneration treated early; recovery is incomplete in those with advanced cord damage.

alternativeTreatments.name: Thorough fish cooking
alternativeTreatments.description: Cooking fish to an internal temperature of at least 63°C reliably kills all Diphyllobothrium plerocercoids. This is the single most effective prevention strategy for people who prepare and consume fish regularly.

alternativeTreatments.name: Commercial-standard freezing
alternativeTreatments.description: Freezing fish at −20°C for 24-48 hours (or −35°C for 15 hours) as required by FDA and EU regulations for fish intended for raw consumption destroys plerocercoid larvae. Home freezers at −18°C may require 7 days of freezing to achieve equivalent kill.

whySeeSpecialist: An infectious disease or tropical medicine specialist should be consulted for confirmation of the diagnosis, access to praziquantel, and co-management of B12 deficiency. A haematologist should be involved if megaloblastic anaemia is severe (haemoglobin below 8 g/dL) or neurological symptoms of B12 deficiency are present. Neurologists are essential for managing subacute combined degeneration, and gastroenterologists may be needed if endoscopic examination or worm extraction is required.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an infectious disease physician with tropical medicine experience who can interpret stool ova and parasite microscopy and prescribe praziquantel. For B12-related complications, choose a haematologist familiar with metabolic causes of macrocytic anaemia who can distinguish diphyllobothriasis-related B12 deficiency from pernicious anaemia. For neurological complications, a neurologist experienced in subacute combined degeneration should supervise B12 repletion and monitor recovery.
preventionStrategies: Cook all freshwater fish and migratory fish (salmon, trout) to an internal temperature of at least 63°C before eating.
Freeze fish intended for raw consumption at −20°C for at least 24-48 hours or −35°C for 15 hours, following food safety regulations for commercial raw fish products.
Do not taste raw fish, fish cakes, or fish tartare during preparation; cook a separate small portion for tasting.
Purchase sushi and sashimi only from reputable establishments that document freeze treatment of fish intended for raw consumption.
Travellers to endemic regions should enquire about fish preparation methods and avoid raw freshwater fish dishes of uncertain provenance.
Treat reservoir animal populations (dogs) that may contaminate lakes and rivers with Diphyllobothrium eggs in intensively used recreational areas.
lifestyleModifications: Replace raw or lightly cured freshwater fish dishes with fully cooked alternatives, or ensure fish is professionally freeze-treated before eating raw.
If you prepare traditional raw fish recipes, invest in a home freezer thermometer and verify that −20°C is achieved for at least 48 hours.
Ensure adequate dietary vitamin B12 intake from cooked meat, poultry, eggs, and dairy; supplement if you follow a plant-based diet.
Inspect stools periodically if you regularly eat raw fish in an endemic area; seek assessment if you notice pale ribbon-like segments.
Report household members who shared the same raw fish meal so they can be assessed and treated if infected.

recommended: Fully cooked freshwater fish (internal temperature ≥63°C) or freeze-certified raw marine fish from regulated suppliers
Vitamin B12-rich foods: liver, beef, eggs, dairy products, and B12-fortified foods — particularly important during recovery from B12 deficiency
Folate-rich foods (dark leafy greens, legumes) to support megaloblastic erythropoiesis recovery
Iron-rich foods if concurrent iron deficiency is identified on blood testing
avoid: Raw, marinated, or lightly cured freshwater fish (gravlax from fresh-caught salmon, raw carp preparations, unsalted fish tartare) unless freeze treatment is documented
Tasting raw fish or fish mixtures during food preparation
Excess alcohol, which independently worsens macrocytic anaemia and impairs B12 absorption
Proton pump inhibitors in high doses if possible, as they reduce gastric acid needed for B12 release from food (relevant during B12 repletion)

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise tolerance may be significantly reduced in patients with severe megaloblastic anaemia, and strenuous activity should be restricted until haemoglobin rises above 9-10 g/dL during recovery. Neurological symptoms from B12 deficiency — unsteady gait, limb weakness — also contraindicate vigorous exercise until neurological improvement is confirmed. Most patients without B12-related complications experience no exercise limitation and can maintain normal activity throughout treatment.
dailyManagement: Take the prescribed single-dose praziquantel with a meal and complete any vitamin B12 supplementation as directed.
Collect a follow-up stool sample at 4-6 weeks and report continued proglottid passage immediately, as a second dose may be needed.
Take B12 supplements consistently until serum B12 is confirmed normal on repeat blood testing at 6-8 weeks.
Report any new tingling, numbness, or unsteadiness promptly — these suggest neurological B12 involvement requiring urgent intramuscular B12 and neurological assessment.
Inform family members or dining companions who shared the same raw fish dish so they can be evaluated.
prognosis: The prognosis of diphyllobothriasis treated with praziquantel is excellent: a single dose achieves parasitological cure in approximately 90-95% of patients, proglottid passage stops within 24-48 hours, and gastrointestinal symptoms resolve quickly. Vitamin B12 deficiency reverses completely with oral supplementation once the competing worm burden is eliminated, and megaloblastic anaemia resolves within 6-12 weeks. Neurological sequelae of prolonged B12 deficiency — subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord — carry a more guarded outlook; recovery occurs in 60-80% of patients treated early, but long-standing cord damage may be permanent. Reinfection after cure is possible and common in endemic communities where raw fish consumption continues, making sustained dietary precautions essential for long-term protection.
recoveryTimeline: Proglottid passage usually stops within 24-48 hours of praziquantel. Stool microscopy should be negative at 4-6 weeks, confirming parasitological cure. Haemoglobin and MCV return toward normal within 6-12 weeks of combined tapeworm treatment and B12 supplementation. Serum B12 normalises within 4-8 weeks. Neurological symptoms, if present, improve over weeks to months with intramuscular B12 but full recovery may take 6-18 months and may be incomplete in advanced disease.
complications: Megaloblastic macrocytic anaemia — from competitive B12 absorption by D. latum; ranges from asymptomatic low B12 to severe symptomatic anaemia requiring treatment.

Subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord — progressive degeneration of posterior and lateral spinal columns from prolonged B12 deficiency, causing limb paraesthesia, gait ataxia, and cognitive changes; potentially permanent if untreated.
Intestinal obstruction — rare complication from a mass of worm segments occluding the small bowel in very heavy infections.
Cholangitis or pancreatitis — rare migration of proglottids into the biliary or pancreatic duct, causing obstructive symptoms.
Nutritional deficiencies beyond B12 — malabsorption of other nutrients including folate in heavy infections contributes to combined nutritional anaemia.
Psychological distress — the discovery of a large tapeworm in the stool causes significant anxiety and, if not promptly addressed, can lead to health anxiety about further parasitic infections.

confusedWithConditions.name: Other intestinal helminthiases
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Diphyllobothriasis produces characteristic wide, flat proglottids in stool and operculated eggs distinct from roundworm, hookworm, or other tapeworm species; it is uniquely associated with B12 deficiency anaemia.

confusedWithConditions.name: Schistosomiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Schistosomiasis causes haematuria or dysentery and hepatic fibrosis from a blood fluke; it has no tapeworm segments in stool, no B12 deficiency, and is acquired through water contact, not fish consumption.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pernicious anaemia (mistaken for)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pernicious anaemia is caused by autoimmune destruction of gastric parietal cells producing intrinsic factor; diphyllobothriasis-related B12 deficiency is caused by competitive worm absorption and resolves completely after praziquantel and B12 repletion, unlike pernicious anaemia which requires lifelong supplementation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Amebiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Amebiasis causes bloody dysentery from colonic ulceration with Entamoeba histolytica trophozoites in stool; no tapeworm segments, no macrocytic anaemia, and it is acquired through contaminated water or food, not fish.

relatedConditions.name: Fascioliasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another foodborne helminthic infection managed with overlapping anthelmintic drugs; represents a different geographic and dietary transmission risk.

relatedConditions.name: Schistosomiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: A trematode infection with overlapping endemic geographies and similar anthelmintic treatment options including praziquantel.

relatedConditions.name: Echinococcosis
relatedConditions.relevance: A cestode (tapeworm) infection with shared anthelmintic drug class; serves as an important differential in patients with abdominal masses found in parasite-endemic regions.

relatedConditions.name: Intestinal helminthiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: The broader category of intestinal worm infections with overlapping diagnostic and treatment approaches.

faqs.question: What is diphyllobothriasis?
faqs.answer: Diphyllobothriasis is an infection by the fish tapeworm (Diphyllobothrium latum and related species) — the longest parasite that infects humans, reaching 10-15 metres. People get it by eating raw or undercooked freshwater fish. Most infections cause only mild symptoms, but the tapeworm can cause vitamin B12 deficiency and anaemia.

faqs.question: How do you get a fish tapeworm?
faqs.answer: Fish tapeworm infection is acquired exclusively by eating raw or undercooked freshwater fish — such as pike, perch, trout, or salmon — that contain infective larvae in their muscle tissue. The infection is not spread from person to person. Sushi, sashimi, gravlax, and raw fish tartares made with freshwater or migratory fish are common sources.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of diphyllobothriasis?
faqs.answer: Most infections are mild or asymptomatic. The most dramatic symptom is passing pale, flat, ribbon-like tapeworm segments in the stool. Other symptoms include mild abdominal discomfort, bloating, nausea, and fatigue. In heavy D. latum infections, vitamin B12 deficiency develops, causing megaloblastic anaemia, profound fatigue, and in severe cases, nerve damage.

faqs.question: Can a fish tapeworm cause anaemia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Diphyllobothrium latum uniquely absorbs vitamin B12 from the host's intestine, depleting the body's B12 stores. This causes megaloblastic macrocytic anaemia — identical to pernicious anaemia — with large, poorly functional red blood cells, pallor, and fatigue. It is one of the defining clinical features that distinguishes D. latum from other tapeworms.

faqs.question: How is diphyllobothriasis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is confirmed by stool microscopy showing the characteristic operculated Diphyllobothrium eggs or by direct examination of passed proglottid segments. Serum vitamin B12 should always be measured. A raised mean corpuscular volume (MCV) and hypersegmented neutrophils on a blood smear suggest concurrent B12 deficiency.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for fish tapeworm?
faqs.answer: A single oral dose of praziquantel 10 mg/kg with a meal cures approximately 90-95% of infections. The tapeworm is usually expelled within 24-48 hours. If B12 deficiency is confirmed, oral B12 supplements (1,000-2,000 mcg daily) are given until levels normalise. A second dose of praziquantel is given if follow-up stool microscopy remains positive at 4-6 weeks.

faqs.question: How long can a fish tapeworm live in the body?
faqs.answer: Diphyllobothrium latum adults can survive in the human intestine for 10-20 years or more without treatment, continuously growing and shedding millions of eggs per day. This prolonged infection is what allows B12 to become progressively depleted in patients who are not diagnosed and treated promptly.

faqs.question: Is diphyllobothriasis contagious?
faqs.answer: No. The fish tapeworm cannot be transmitted from person to person. The infection requires eating infected fish because the larval stage that infects humans develops inside freshwater fish, not in the environment or in other people.

faqs.question: Can sushi give you a tapeworm?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the sushi uses raw freshwater fish or migratory species (such as Pacific salmon) that carry Diphyllobothrium larvae and have not been freeze-treated. Commercial sushi-grade fish regulations in the United States and EU require freezing at temperatures that kill tapeworm larvae, but artisan or home-prepared raw fish dishes may not comply.

faqs.question: How does freezing prevent fish tapeworm infection?
faqs.answer: Freezing fish at −20°C for 24-48 hours, or at −35°C for 15 hours, kills all Diphyllobothrium plerocercoid larvae in the muscle. Home freezers set to −18°C require approximately 7 days of freezing for the same effect. Standard home refrigerator temperatures do not kill the larvae.

faqs.question: What does a tapeworm segment look like in stool?
faqs.answer: Diphyllobothrium proglottids are pale yellowish-white, flat, and ribbon-like segments wider than they are long (typically 10-15 mm wide and 3-5 mm long). They may be passed singly, in a chain, or as a long ribbon. They are sometimes still moving when first passed and can cause significant distress to the patient.

faqs.question: Can diphyllobothriasis damage the nervous system?
faqs.answer: Yes, in severe cases. Prolonged B12 deficiency from untreated D. latum infection can cause subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord — progressive damage to the nerve tracts in the spinal cord — producing tingling and numbness in the hands and feet, unsteady walking, and cognitive changes. This is a serious complication that requires urgent B12 treatment.

faqs.question: How big does a fish tapeworm get?
faqs.answer: Diphyllobothrium latum is the longest tapeworm that infects humans: adult worms typically reach 3-10 metres in length and have been reported at up to 25 metres. Despite this extraordinary size, most infected people experience only mild symptoms because the tapeworm does not invade tissues.

faqs.question: Does diphyllobothriasis affect the liver?
faqs.answer: No. The fish tapeworm lives exclusively in the small intestine. Rarely, proglottids migrate into the bile duct and cause cholangitis or into the pancreatic duct and cause pancreatitis, but these are uncommon complications. Liver involvement is not part of the typical clinical picture of diphyllobothriasis.

faqs.question: How do I know if I have a fish tapeworm if I have no symptoms?
faqs.answer: Many people with fish tapeworm infection have no symptoms and discover the infection only when they pass a segment in the stool or when a blood test reveals unexplained macrocytic anaemia or low B12. Anyone who regularly eats raw freshwater fish and develops unexplained fatigue or macrocytic anaemia should mention their dietary habits to their doctor.

faqs.question: Is it safe to eat raw salmon?
faqs.answer: Commercially prepared sushi-grade salmon from regulated suppliers in the U.S., EU, and Japan is generally freeze-treated and safe. Wild-caught salmon prepared at home or from artisan sources carries a real risk of Diphyllobothrium infection unless it has been properly frozen. Cooking salmon to 63°C internal temperature eliminates all risk.

faqs.question: Can I get rid of a tapeworm naturally?
faqs.answer: No effective natural remedy for Diphyllobothrium tapeworm infection exists. Praziquantel or niclosamide are required for reliable cure. Delaying treatment allows the worm to continue depleting B12 and growing longer in the intestine. Seek medical care as soon as a tapeworm infection is suspected.

faqs.question: Can children get fish tapeworm?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children who eat raw or undercooked freshwater fish can develop diphyllobothriasis. B12 deficiency and its neurological consequences can be particularly serious in children, where it may impair cognitive development. Cases have been reported in children as young as toddlers who were given raw fish as a family food.

faqs.question: How long does it take to feel better after tapeworm treatment?
faqs.answer: Proglottid passage typically stops within 24-48 hours of praziquantel. Gastrointestinal symptoms like bloating and abdominal discomfort resolve within days. Megaloblastic anaemia takes 6-12 weeks to correct with B12 supplementation. Neurological symptoms from B12 deficiency improve over weeks to months with intramuscular B12 injections.

faqs.question: Does praziquantel kill the entire tapeworm?
faqs.answer: Praziquantel destroys the scolex (head) and tegument of the tapeworm, which leads to the worm being digested in the intestine rather than passed intact. You may notice worm fragments in the stool for 1-2 days after treatment. The treatment is considered successful if stool microscopy is negative for eggs at 4-6 weeks after treatment.

metaDescription: Diphyllobothriasis is a fish tapeworm infection from raw fish causing B12 deficiency and anaemia. Learn symptoms, diagnosis, and praziquantel treatment.
